title: Sunday, September 24
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
September 24, 2017 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Seventeenth Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time, falling within the long green season of the church year. The RCL readings for this Sunday (Year A) would be Exodus 16:2-15 (manna in the wilderness), Psalm 105:37-45, Philippians 1:21-30 (Paul on life and death), and Matthew 20:1-16 (the Parable of the Laborers in the Vineyard). The gospel's theme of grace, fairness, and God's generosity would have been the primary theological focus for Sunday preaching.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of General Convention 2018 planning, with significant internal debate over sexuality and marriage equality still ongoing despite the church's progressive stances. In the Diocese of Colorado, Bishop Robert O'Neill was serving during a period of relative stability, though the national church continued wrestling with declining membership and the integration of contemporary social justice movements into parish life. Katie Pearson, as a female priest (women's ordination fully normalized in ECUSA by 2017), represented the church's evolution on gender in leadership.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical late September weather with the first hints of autumn arriving. The city's downtown core, where Saint John's Cathedral sits on 17th Avenue near Civic Center Park, was occupied by an active urban renewal period with ongoing Capitol Hill gentrification affecting long-term residents. Local focus was on state legislative issues and preparation for the 2018 election cycle, with Colorado politics tilting Democratic but housing affordability and development tensions high in the metro area.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

Hurricane Maria was intensifying toward Puerto Rico and would make landfall on September 20, 2017, becoming the second-strongest hurricane of the season and devastating the island. President Trump's NFL anthem controversy was at fever pitch, with his September 22 comments about players kneeling for the national anthem sparking massive counter-protests and player activism. The Trump administration was continuing efforts to repeal the Affordable Care Act, with Senator John McCain's vote against the skincare repeal just days past (September 28 McCain vote was still upcoming). Social movements around racial justice and police violence remained prominent national concerns in the year following 2016 election turbulence.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Myanmar's military was conducting a brutal crackdown against the Rohingya Muslim minority, with tens of thousands fleeing into Bangladesh in what was beginning to be recognized as ethnic cleansing; this humanitarian crisis was building international pressure and media attention. North Korea had recently conducted its sixth nuclear test on September 3, 2017, escalating tensions with the Trump administration's increasingly aggressive rhetoric. The aftermath of the Barcelona terror attack (August 17) and broader European security concerns around migration and extremism remained in the news cycle.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was deeply polarized and anxious, with the Trump presidency dominating headlines daily and cultural divisions playing out vividly through social media and cable news. The NFL anthem protests were dividing Americans along partisan lines, touching on race, patriotism, and national identity in ways that reached every social institution including churches. In Denver, the Broncos' 2017 season was underway (they would struggle that year), and sports remained a significant cultural touchstone even amid broader social turbulence. The zeitgeist was one of strain and contestation over fundamental American values.
